documents.content: The 14 algorithmic patterns that cover ~80% of coding interview questions: (1) Two Pointers — sorted arrays, pair sum problems. (2) Sliding Window — substring/subarray with constraints. (3) Fast and Slow Pointers — cycle detection, finding middle. (4) Merge Intervals — overlapping intervals, scheduling. (5) Cyclic Sort — when array contains 1 to N. (6) In-place Reversal of Linked List. (7) Tree BFS — level-order traversal. (8) Tree DFS — path sum, lowest common ancestor. (9) Two Heaps — find median, sliding window median. (10) Subsets — backtracking, permutations. (11) Modified Binary Search — rotated arrays, search in answer space. (12) Top K Elements — heap-based selection. (13) K-way Merge — merge K sorted lists, smallest range. (14) Dynamic Programming — knapsack, longest common subsequence, edit distance. Master one pattern per week with 5-10 practice problems each.

How to approach a coding interview question — a framework that works: (1) Repeat the problem back in your own words. This catches misunderstandings early and shows active listening. (2) Walk through 1-2 concrete examples by hand. The interviewer will correct you if your understanding is wrong. (3) Identify edge cases: empty input, single element, negative numbers, duplicates, very large input. (4) Discuss a brute-force solution first with its time/space complexity, even if obviously not optimal. This proves you can solve the problem at all. (5) Optimize — usually by identifying repeated work, using a hash map, or finding a smarter data structure. (6) Code it carefully. Use meaningful variable names. (7) Trace through your code with one example. (8) Discuss complexity and potential improvements. The candidates who skip steps 1-5 and dive straight into code usually struggle.

Things that fail coding interviews even when you 'solve' the problem: writing code with no comments or explanation, getting defensive when the interviewer challenges your solution, refusing to consider hints, brute-forcing through optimization without thinking, not testing your code with the examples you discussed, hardcoding return values to pass test cases, panicking and rewriting from scratch instead of debugging in place, using language features the interviewer hasn't seen (don't pull out Python's bisect_left if you can't explain it), and not asking clarifying questions when the problem is ambiguous. Communication is half the score in any coding interview — your code being correct is the floor, not the ceiling.
